title: The Desert Mathematics
subtitle: Counting the Dunes
storyContext: Qamar challenges the player to a game of wits. Before she shares her stolen magic, the player must prove they can count the grains of sand—or at least the basic numbers.
openingMonologue: Oh, another traveler? Zafar must have sent you. Before I share my stolen magic, let's see if you can even count the dunes. Learn these numbers (أرقام - arqām), and maybe I won't trick you today!

title: The Fox's Etiquette
subtitle: Words of Courtesy
storyContext: Qamar is a trickster, but even tricksters have rules. She teaches the player how to properly greet friends and foes in the desert.
openingMonologue: You survived the numbers, but your manners are atrocious! Even a desert fox knows how to properly greet (تحية - taḥiyya) a rival. Let me teach you some basic courtesy before you embarrass us both.

title: Anatomy of a Trickster
subtitle: Parts of the Body
storyContext: Qamar points out the player's physical flaws compared to her magnificent glowing ears, teaching body parts in the process.
openingMonologue: Look at you—your ears are so small and your eyes lack any magical glow! Still, I suppose you need to know the parts of the body (جسم - jism) to cast a proper spell. Pay attention, human!

title: Secrets of the Sands
subtitle: Elements of Nature
storyContext: Qamar reveals the elemental magic of Al-Rashid, teaching the vocabulary of nature that the ancient sorcerers used to control the environment.
openingMonologue: The sorcerers of Al-Rashid didn't just read books; they commanded the very elements of nature (طبيعة - ṭabīʿa). If you want to survive the desert, you must learn the names of the sun, moon, and fire.

title: The Verbs of Power
subtitle: Words of Action
storyContext: Qamar finally decides to share the true words of action. She teaches the player essential verbs, the core of any magical incantation.
openingMonologue: We have reached the true core of magic: action! To do anything in this world, you must master the verbs (أفعال - afʿāl) of power. Learn these, and you might actually be of use to me.
